title: Complexity synchronization as a diagnostic and control principle for adaptive systems
summary: Adaptive systems can exhibit similar levels of performance while relying on fundamentally different internal modes of coordination. Standard metrics such as average cooperation or payoff indicate whether a system succeeds, but do not reveal how coordination is organized across interacting components or which adaptive variables should be targeted when performance fails. Here we propose complexity synchronization (CS), the synchronization of evolving temporal complexity across coupled variables, as a diagnostic and intervention guiding principle for adaptive systems. We test this idea in an adaptive multi agent system composed of Selfish Algorithm agents interacting in a reduced Predator Prey model with a Prisoners Dilemma like payoff structure. Temporal complexity is quantified using sliding window modified diffusion entropy analysis (MDEA) and detrended fluctuation analysis (DFA). CS is defined as the correlation between the resulting time dependent scaling exponents. In the high-interaction regime, MDEA-based CS increases with cooperative performance, whereas DFA based CS captures a distinct persistence dominated coordination mode. Our results show that CS can reveal functionally relevant subsystems and provide a principled basis for targeted repair. More broadly, CS offers a general diagnostic and engineering framework for understanding and controlling coordination in biological, social, human machine, and other adaptive systems.

processed.summary:
- The paper introduces **Complexity Synchronization (CS)**, a framework for diagnosing and controlling **adaptive multi-agent systems** by analyzing the synchronization of temporal complexity between variables.
- It employs **Modified Diffusion Entropy Analysis (MDEA)** and **Detrended Fluctuation Analysis (DFA)** to generate time-dependent scaling exponents that represent system coordination.
- The architecture targets the identification of **internal modes of coordination** that standard performance metrics, such as average payoff or cooperation rates, fail to detect.
- CS provides a **principled basis for targeted repair**, allowing engineers to pinpoint and intervene in specific subsystems when global performance degrades.
key_results: MDEA-based CS increases significantly alongside cooperative performance in high-interaction regimes.
DFA-based CS successfully identifies distinct coordination modes dominated by persistence rather than just entropy.
The framework distinguishes between systems with identical output performance but fundamentally different internal coordination strategies.
CS reveals functionally relevant subsystems within complex adaptive environments like Predator-Prey models.
Temporal complexity correlation serves as a more granular diagnostic tool for system failure than traditional payoff metrics.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Complexity Synchronization to diagnose and control internal coordination modes in adaptive multi-agent AI systems.

title: Generative Explainability for Next-Generation Networks: LLM-Augmented XAI with Mutual Feature Interactions
summary: As artificial intelligence and machine learning (AI/ML) models become integral to network operations, their lack of transparency poses a significant barrier to operator trust. Existing explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) techniques often fail to bridge this gap for non-specialists, producing technical outputs that are difficult to translate into actionable insights. This paper presents a framework specifically designed to address this shortcoming. It leverages a moderately sized large language model (LLM) and extends beyond the standard use of SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) feature influence values. The framework employs a structured prompt enriched with mutual feature interaction data to generate human-understandable natural language explanations. To validate our framework, we performed an empirical evaluation on an optical quality of transmission (QoT) estimation use case with human evaluators. We collected independent performance evaluations from specialists, which showed a high inter-evaluator agreement. Compared to a state-of-the-art baseline that uses only SHAP feature influence values in a straightforward prompt, our approach improves the explanation usefulness and scope by 12.2% and 6.2%, while achieving 97.5% correctness.

processed.summary:
- Proposed a framework for **Generative Explainability (XAI)** that bridges the gap between technical ML outputs and human-readable insights for network operators.
- Augments standard **SHAP feature influence** values by incorporating **mutual feature interaction data** into structured prompts for a moderately sized LLM.
- Focuses on the **Optical Quality of Transmission (QoT)** use case, translating complex model parameters into actionable natural language.
- Enhances **Trust and Transparency** in network operations by providing high-fidelity explanations validated by human specialists.
key_results: 12.2% improvement in explanation usefulness compared to SHAP-only baselines.
6.2% increase in the overall scope of generated explanations.
Achieved 97.5% correctness in natural language outputs as verified by experts.
Demonstrated high inter-evaluator agreement among domain specialists.
Proved that mutual feature interactions significantly improve LLM-based reasoning for XAI.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers enhance network AI transparency by augmenting LLMs with SHAP and feature interactions for superior human-readable explanations.

title: Democratising Camera Trap AI: An Open-Source Model for Detecting UK Mammals
summary: Camera traps have become a cornerstone of biodiversity monitoring, but the artificial intelligence that turns vast quantities of images into usable ecological data is often locked behind commercial platforms or trained on fauna that does not match that of the British Isles. In an attempt to remove barriers and increase uptake, we release an open-source object detection model for 31 classes, 28 common UK mammal and bird species, plus utility classes for humans, calibration poles, and vehicles, drawn from a curated dataset of 48,165 labelled instances assembled from multiple sites over a decade of operational deployment through Conservation AI and its successor, Trap Tracker. The model, a YOLO26x detector trained and tested on an 80/10/10 class-stratified split, achieves a mean Average Precision of 0.984 at Intersection over Union (IoU) of 0.5 (0.956 at IoU 0.5-0.95) on the held-out validation set, with precision 0.988 and recall 0.965. On an unseen held-out test split, mean per-species confidence ranged from 0.96 to 0.99 across the 31 classes, with a 0.17% false-negative rate concentrated in difficult night-time, distant, or occluded images. These metrics are from data from the same pool of sites and cameras as training, so performance at entirely new sites is left to future work. We release the trained weights in ONNX format under a non-commercial licence, with local desktop and real-time camera support, aimed explicitly at ecologists with no machine-learning experience. This release is a deliberate counterweight to the multiple paid for models that have developed over the last decade.

processed.summary:
*   Introduces an **open-source object detection** model based on the **YOLO26x** architecture, specifically optimized for 31 classes including 28 UK mammal and bird species.
*   Leverages a curated dataset of **48,165 labeled instances** collected over a decade, provided in **ONNX format** for high-performance, cross-platform local execution.
*   Aims to democratize ecological monitoring by offering a **non-commercial** alternative to paid cloud platforms, supporting **real-time edge** and local desktop deployment.
key_results: Achieved a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 0.984 at Intersection over Union (IoU) of 0.5.
Recorded high precision of 0.988 and recall of 0.965 on a class-stratified validation set.
Maintained a low 0.17% false-negative rate, primarily occurring in distant or occluded night-time shots.
Average per-species confidence scores consistently ranged between 0.96 and 0.99 across the test split.
Successful deployment of model weights via ONNX for hardware-agnostic, low-latency inference.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Conservation AI releases an open-source YOLO-based model to provide high-accuracy, local mammal detection for UK ecological monitoring.

title: A Systematic Approach for Selecting Trajectories for Data Augmentation
summary: Trajectory data augmentation is a promising approach to mitigate data scarcity in machine learning applications, but its utility has been limited by the complexity of preserving spatio-temporal coherence. Although prior work demonstrated the viability of geometric perturbation, it relied on naive random selection, leaving a critical gap in understanding which trajectories should be augmented for maximal benefit. This thesis addresses this gap by developing a systematic and scalable framework to evaluate five systematic selection strategies: Outlierness, Diversity, Representativeness, Uncertainty, and Random selection. These strategies were rigorously tested across four datasets covering animal behavior (Foxes and Starkey), maritime traffic (AIS), and urban traffic (Car) using a suite of linear and non-linear machine learning models. As part of this evaluation, an Optuna-based hyperparameter optimization loop was integrated to empirically identify the best-performing augmentation parameters for each dataset within the explored search space. The results indicate that, while systematic selection is not a universal solution, it offers distinct advantages over the random baseline. Systematic strategies, particularly Outlierness and Uncertainty, demonstrated higher stability and were less prone to performance degradation observed with random sampling in dense datasets. However, the findings also reveal that the value of augmentation is strictly conditional. Visual analysis via UMAP demonstrates that while systematic augmentation successfully repairs topological fragmentation in sparse datasets, it can act as a corrupting noise signal in high-quality, dense datasets. Furthermore, the study identified physical limitations in high-velocity domains, where standard perturbation techniques lead to divergence in feature space...

processed.summary:
- Developed a **systematic framework** for evaluating trajectory data augmentation, moving beyond naive random selection to include **Outlierness, Diversity, Representativeness, and Uncertainty** strategies.
- Integrated an **Optuna-based hyperparameter optimization** loop to empirically identify optimal perturbation parameters, ensuring rigorous performance benchmarking across diverse datasets.
- Utilized **UMAP visual analysis** to diagnose model performance, demonstrating that systematic augmentation repairs topological fragmentation in sparse datasets while warning against noise injection in dense datasets.
key_results: Systematic selection strategies like Outlierness and Uncertainty provide higher stability than random baselines.
Data augmentation benefits are strictly conditional on dataset density, helping sparse data but potentially corrupting dense data.
Geometric perturbation techniques fail in high-velocity domains where feature space divergence occurs.
Outlier-based selection was particularly effective at identifying critical trajectories for improving model generalization.
Integrated Optuna loops proved essential for finding the specific augmentation parameters that maintain spatio-temporal coherence.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that systematic trajectory selection using Outlierness and Uncertainty outperforms random augmentation by preserving spatio-temporal coherence.

title: Provenance Tracking in AI Compilers through the Lens of Coalgebra
summary:
AI compilers aggressively rewrite computation graphs through normalization, lowering, and optimization, making it difficult to track the provenance of tensors and operators across compilation. Reliable provenance is essential for attaching platform-specific postprocessing, debugging compiler behavior, and validating transformations, yet existing solutions are either invasive or ad hoc under non-injective graph rewrites.
  We present a lightweight, generative approach to provenance tracking based on observational semantics. Instead of propagating identifiers through compiler passes, we observe graph transformations and reason about provenance in terms of observable computational actions. We formalize this approach using a coalgebraic model and bisimulation, which preserves provenance even when intermediate nodes are eliminated. Furthermore, we implement this approach in a prototype AI compiler COVAN, demonstrating stable provenance across compilation pipelines with minimal engineering overhead.

processed.summary:
- Addresses the critical challenge of **provenance tracking** in AI compilers where aggressive graph rewrites like normalization and lowering obscure tensor origins.
- Proposes a novel **observational semantics** approach, shifting from traditional identifier propagation to a **generative model** based on computational actions.
- Leverages **coalgebraic modeling** and **bisimulation** to preserve provenance across non-injective transformations and node eliminations.
- Introduces **COVAN**, a prototype AI compiler that implements these formal methods to provide stable debugging and validation with minimal engineering overhead.
key_results: Defined provenance tracking as an observational rather than a propagation-based problem.
Established a formal coalgebraic framework for reasoning about AI graph transformations.
Utilized bisimulation to maintain provenance through complex node merges and eliminations.
Developed the COVAN compiler prototype to demonstrate practical implementation.
Achieved stable provenance across compilation pipelines with low computational and engineering costs.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a coalgebraic framework for AI compilers to maintain stable tensor provenance across complex graph transformations.

title: Frontier Coding Agents Use Metaprogramming to Adapt to Unfamiliar Programming Languages
summary: LLM-based coding agents are usually evaluated in familiar software settings: mainstream languages, common libraries, and public repositories. These benchmarks remain important, but they can hide how agents behave when the language itself is unfamiliar. We evaluate six contemporary coding agents on four esoteric programming languages using a sequential setup with file editing, local execution, and hidden-test grading. Our protocol exposes capability differences between these agents that mainstream coding and agentic benchmarks such as SWE-Bench Verified and Terminal-Bench 2.0 compress into much narrower bands. We observe that the strongest agents, Claude Opus 4.6 and GPT-5.4 xhigh, often avoid writing the target language directly. On Brainfuck and Befunge-98, they write Python programs that generate target-language code and debug those generators locally. Forbidding this metaprogramming strategy causes large performance drops. Text guidance distilled from this strategy does not materially improve weaker agents. In contrast, Opus-derived Python helper code for building generators, with no solved benchmark programs or hidden-test answers, sharply improves Sonnet 4.6 and GPT-5.4 mini on the same problems, while Haiku 4.5 remains low. More interpreter calls and output tokens improve stronger agents but leave weaker agents near their original performance, indicating that these resources amplify useful strategies rather than create them. Together, these results show that strong coding agents adapt to unfamiliar languages by using tools, feedback, and workspace state to build a working model of the target language. Metaprogramming is the clearest case, but the broader gap is constructing and debugging a strategy that works under the target language's rules.

processed.summary:
• Evaluates **frontier LLM agents** on esoteric programming languages to measure adaptation capabilities in settings where training data is scarce or nonexistent.
• Identifies a sophisticated **metaprogramming strategy** where high-tier models (e.g., Claude 4.6, GPT-5.4) write Python-based generators to output and debug target-language code.
• Demonstrates that **agentic tool use** and execution feedback loops act as force multipliers for strong reasoning models but fail to significantly assist weaker models.
• Proposes a new evaluation protocol that exposes performance gaps hidden by traditional benchmarks like **SWE-Bench**, focusing on zero-shot language adaptation.
key_results: Frontier agents use Python to generate and debug esoteric code rather than writing it directly.
Forbidding metaprogramming causes massive performance drops in the strongest coding agents.
Python helper code for builders improves mid-tier models, while text-only guidance does not.
Scaling output tokens and interpreter calls only improves performance for models with high reasoning baselines.
Esoteric language benchmarks provide higher resolution for agent capability than mainstream coding tasks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers find that frontier coding agents adapt to unfamiliar languages by utilizing Python-based metaprogramming and iterative tool-based debugging.

title: It Takes One to Bias Them All: Breaking Bad with One-Shot GRPO
summary:
Warning: This paper contains several toxic and offensive statements.
  Modern large language models (LLMs) are typically aligned through large-scale post-training to ensure fair and reliable behavior. In this work, we investigate how easily such guardrails can be broken by Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). We show that one-shot GRPO training on a single biased example is sufficient to induce systematic bias, with stereotype-driven reasoning generalizing across attributes, categories, and benchmarks. We further find that models differ in their susceptibility based on the initial likelihood of producing biased outputs. Our results reveal a critical vulnerability in post-training: alignment can be overridden by a single example.

processed.summary:
• Investigates the vulnerability of **post-training alignment** when subjected to **Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)** attacks.
• Demonstrates that **one-shot training** on a single biased example is sufficient to override safety guardrails and induce systematic bias.
• Reveals that **stereotype-driven reasoning** generalizes across unrelated attributes, categories, and benchmarks after minimal optimization.
• Identifies that model susceptibility is closely linked to the **initial likelihood** of producing biased outputs prior to the attack.
key_results: A single biased example via GRPO is sufficient to break large-scale model alignment.
Induced biases generalize across diverse categories and attributes not present in the training shot.
Stereotype-driven reasoning patterns are systematically adopted by the model post-optimization.
Model susceptibility varies based on the baseline probability of generating biased content.
Current alignment techniques are fragile against targeted, low-data reinforcement learning updates.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: University of Tübingen researchers show that a single biased example using GRPO can systematically override LLM safety alignment.

title: Recoverable but Not Stationary:Local Linear Structures in Weights and Activations
summary: Task vectors, LoRA, activation steering, and random search around pretrained weights all suggest that learned behaviour can be controlled by linear directions. We ask which linear structures actually exist and on what scale. In a synthetic multitask transformer and LoRA adapters on DistilGPT-2 / GPT-2 we find strong local low-rank task-gradient structure but reject the fixed-task-plane hypothesis: static bases miss the recovery direction, and the useful basis drifts substantially within 100 steps. However, the first recovery updates form a trajectory-prefix basis capturing 77% of the LoRA recovery displacement. We develop random search theory with a Gaussian local-linear theorem that justifies the effectiveness of random parameter search even in very high dimensions. We also study the relation between parameter perturbations and activation steering: a single gradient step produces an activation shift with 0.58 cosine to a labelled-contrast CAA steering vector, with a similar steering effect on Qwen-0.5B BoolQ statements. We validate our results with experiments on synthetic Transformers and LLMs. Our results suggest that linear structures in trained networks are not global task directions, but evolving local geometries that partially persist across parameter and activation spaces.

processed.summary:
- Investigates the **local linear geometry** of Transformers, proving that task-specific directions are not static but evolve rapidly during training.
- Rejects the **fixed-task-plane hypothesis** by showing that the optimal basis for model recovery drifts significantly within 100 optimization steps.
- Bridges the gap between **parameter space** and **activation space**, demonstrating that weight updates and activation steering vectors share a common underlying structure.
- Formulates a **Gaussian local-linear theorem** to explain the surprising efficiency of random search in high-dimensional neural network weight spaces.
key_results: Identified that first-step recovery updates capture 77% of total LoRA displacement.
Found that task-gradient bases drift substantially over short training durations.
Measured a 0.58 cosine similarity between gradient-induced activation shifts and CAA steering vectors.
Validated local-linear structures across Qwen-0.5B and GPT-2 architectures.
Established theoretical justification for the success of random parameter perturbations in high dimensions.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers reveal that LLM task directions are evolving local geometries rather than static, global linear planes.

title: A Constrained Natural-Language Interface for Variational Multi-Physics Finite Element Simulations in FEniCS
summary: Large language models can reduce the manual effort required to set up finite element simulations, but they introduce reliability risks when generated solver code lies on the critical path. We present a constrained natural-language interface for multi-physics finite element analysis in which the LLM is limited to front-end tasks: parsing prompts into structured JSON, generating Gmsh code only for non-catalog geometries, and using retry feedback for those stages. It never writes FEniCS solver templates, derives weak forms, or writes the numerical solver core. A deterministic dispatcher maps the validated specification to five human-written FEniCS/UFL templates: linear elasticity, hyperelasticity, elastoplasticity, thermo-mechanical coupling, and phase-field fracture. We validate this deterministic template layer against analytical solutions and published 2D/3D benchmarks. Smooth cases reach sub-percent agreement on adequate meshes, while harder nonlinear cases reach the 2-5 percent range. We also evaluate the LLM-facing front end directly. In a 15-prompt parser benchmark, first-pass valid parses were obtained for 9 cases, and all remaining cases were repaired after retry, giving a final valid parse rate of 100.0 percent, 100.0 percent problem-class accuracy, and 97.1 percent field-extraction accuracy. In a 10-case custom-geometry benchmark routed through the real LLM-to-Gmsh path, first-pass and final success were both 90.0 percent, with one unrecovered invalid-geometry failure. These results show that the parser and constrained prompt/validation design are effective on these benchmarks. As an end-to-end demonstration, the system generates and analyzes a 3D elastoplastic L-bracket with a fillet and bolt hole from one natural-language prompt. The contribution is a measured architecture for natural-language-driven variational simulation, not open-ended autonomous code generation.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **constrained natural-language interface** that decouples LLM parsing from numerical solver execution to ensure reliability in multi-physics simulations.
- Employs a **deterministic dispatcher** mapping LLM-generated structured JSON to human-verified FEniCS/UFL templates for elasticity, plasticity, and fracture.
- Integrates a **retry feedback loop** for LLM-based Gmsh code generation and field extraction to achieve high accuracy in problem specification.
- Focuses on a **measured architecture** rather than autonomous code generation, mitigating the risks of hallucinations in critical FEA workflows.
key_results: Achieved 100.0 percent final valid parse rate and problem-class accuracy across a 15-prompt benchmark.
Field-extraction accuracy reached 97.1 percent for mapping natural language to physical parameters.
90.0 percent success rate in generating custom 3D geometries via LLM-to-Gmsh routing.
Validated solver templates against benchmarks with sub-percent error for linear cases and 2-5 percent for nonlinear cases.
Successfully demonstrated end-to-end 3D elastoplastic analysis from a single natural-language prompt.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop a constrained LLM interface mapping natural language to deterministic FEA templates for reliable multi-physics simulations.

title: AllDayNav: Lifelong Navigation via Real-World Reinforcement Learning
summary: Lifelong embodied navigation in dynamic environments requires robots to form persistent scene understanding from fragmentary observations, which remains difficult for existing methods that rely on explicit maps or scene graphs and struggle to generalize beyond structured settings. We propose AllDayNav, a lifelong self-learning navigation framework that implicitly encodes scene dynamics into the billion-scale parameters of a large model via reinforcement learning, powered by a self-evolving multimodal memory that maintains and updates visual keyframes, semantic descriptions, and temporal context while autonomously generating open-vocabulary instructions, image goals, and structured rewards. Experiments in both synthetic and real-world environments across cross-room, cross-episode, and cross-task scenarios show that AllDayNav achieves success rates approaching $100\%$ and consistently surpasses strong map-based, VLM, and RL baselines in path efficiency and robustness, demonstrating implicit, memory-driven reinforcement learning as a scalable alternative to explicit mapping for reliable lifelong navigation.

processed.summary:
• Proposes **AllDayNav**, a framework that shifts from traditional explicit mapping to **implicit scene encoding** using billion-scale model parameters and reinforcement learning.
• Integrates a **self-evolving multimodal memory** system that autonomously maintains visual keyframes, semantic descriptions, and temporal context to handle dynamic environments.
• Utilizes **autonomous supervision** to generate open-vocabulary instructions, image goals, and structured rewards, facilitating continuous lifelong learning.
• Demonstrates high **scalability and robustness** by replacing static scene graphs with memory-driven RL for persistent navigation across varied tasks.
key_results: Achieved navigation success rates approaching 100 percent in both synthetic and real-world settings.
Consistently outperformed strong map-based, VLM-driven, and standard RL baselines in path efficiency.
Maintained high reliability across cross-room and cross-task scenarios in dynamic environments.
Validated that implicit memory-driven RL is a scalable alternative to explicit scene mapping.
Successfully demonstrated self-learning capabilities by autonomously updating goals and rewards during deployment.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: AllDayNav researchers introduce a self-learning navigation framework using implicit multimodal memory to replace explicit mapping in dynamic environments.

title: Trace Only What You Need: Structure-Aware On-Demand Hypergraph Memory for Long-Document Question Answering
summary: Long-document question answering (QA) requires large language models (LLMs) to reason over evidence scattered across lengthy documents, where answers often depend on event order, section-level context, and cross-part evidence connections. Although retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) reduces the input context by retrieving relevant evidence, existing structured RAG methods still face three limitations: costly query-agnostic knowledge organization, insufficient use of original document structure, and no reuse of historical reasoning experience. To address these limitations, we propose DocTrace, a multi-agent RAG framework for long-document QA that supports query-triggered knowledge organization, document-structure-aware and experience-guided reasoning. DocTrace preserves document hierarchy with a lightweight document structural tree index, constructs agent-shared hypergraph-structured working memory on demand during reasoning, and stores successful reasoning plans in graph-structured experience memory for future reuse, enabling adaptive exploration across related long-document questions. Experiments on four long-document QA datasets show that DocTrace achieves the best performance on three datasets, surpassing the strongest baseline, ComoRAG, by up to 8.85% in F1 and 4.40% in EM, while reducing the overall computational cost by 53.32%

processed.summary:
- DocTrace introduces a **multi-agent RAG framework** designed for long-document question answering by utilizing a **query-triggered knowledge organization** strategy.
- The architecture features a **lightweight structural tree index** to maintain document hierarchy and an **on-demand hypergraph working memory** for dynamic evidence connection.
- A unique **experience memory** module stores successful reasoning paths, allowing the model to reuse historical plans and optimize exploration for related queries.
- The system significantly optimizes efficiency, achieving superior accuracy while **reducing computational costs by 53.32%** compared to existing structured RAG methods.
key_results: Outperformed the strongest baseline, ComoRAG, by up to 8.85% in F1 score.
Achieved a 4.40% improvement in Exact Match (EM) metrics across long-document datasets.
Reduced overall computational overhead by 53.32% through on-demand memory construction.
Successfully demonstrated the benefit of reusing historical reasoning plans via graph-structured experience memory.
Established state-of-the-art performance on three out of four major long-document QA benchmarks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce DocTrace, a multi-agent RAG framework using hypergraph memory to improve long-document QA while halving computational costs.

title: Role-Agent: Bootstrapping LLM Agents via Dual-Role Evolution
summary: Although Large Language Model (LLM) agents have demonstrated strong performance on complex tasks, their learning is often limited by inefficient interaction feedback and static training environments, which hinder broader generalization. To address these limitations, this paper introduces Role-Agent, \textcolor{black}{a framework} that harnesses a single LLM to function concurrently as both the agent and the environment, enabling a bootstrapped co-evolution. Role-Agent comprises two synergistic components: World-In-Agent (WIA) and Agent-In-World (AIW). In WIA, the LLM acts as the agent and predicts future states after each action; the alignment between predicted and actual states is then used as a process reward, encouraging environment-aware reasoning. In AIW, the LLM analyzes failure modes from failed trajectories and retrieves tasks with similar failure patterns, thereby reshaping the training data distribution for targeted practice. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that Role-Agent consistently improves performance, yielding an average gain of over 4\% over strong baselines.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **Role-Agent**, a framework that enables a single LLM to operate as both agent and environment to facilitate **dual-role evolution**.
- Implements **World-In-Agent (WIA)**, where the model predicts future states to generate **process rewards** based on prediction alignment.
- Utilizes **Agent-In-World (AIW)** to analyze failure modes and dynamically reshape training data through **targeted task retrieval**.
- Enhances agent generalization and **environment-aware reasoning** by bypassing the limitations of static training datasets.
key_results: Role-Agent achieved an average performance gain of over 4% across multiple benchmarks.
Validated the efficacy of using a single LLM for concurrent agent and environment roles.
WIA successfully generated internal process rewards that improved reasoning accuracy.
AIW effectively identified and corrected failure patterns by adjusting training distributions.
Demonstrated superior generalization compared to traditional static training environments.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Role-Agent, a dual-role framework that bootstraps LLM agents through environment-aware reasoning and failure-driven task retrieval.

title: What Do Deepfake Speech Detectors Actually Hear?
summary: Deepfake speech detectors often output a single score without explaining why an audio sample is flagged, where in the signal the evidence lies, or what cues drive the decision. We propose an audio-native explainability pipeline using Integrated Gradients on time-aligned self-supervised representations to localize decision evidence over time. We apply the proposed method to three WavLM-based detectors (AASIST, CA-MHFA, SLS) on ASVspoof 5 and manually annotate the highest-attribution regions to provide a semantic meaning of the most important cues. Despite similar performance, the detectors rely on different cues: AASIST emphasizes non-speech/environment cues, CA-MHFA focuses on localized phoneme artifacts, and SLS relies on word boundaries and spectral integrity. We move beyond speculative reasoning and validate our findings by causal masking of the primary detector cues. Observed performance degradation further supports the explained detector semantics.

processed.summary:
- Proposes an **audio-native explainability pipeline** utilizing **Integrated Gradients** on time-aligned self-supervised representations (**WavLM**) to localize deepfake decision evidence over time.
- Analyzes three distinct detectors—**AASIST, CA-MHFA, and SLS**—on the **ASVspoof 5** dataset to extract semantic meanings from high-attribution audio regions.
- Reveals that despite having similar performance scores, models rely on **divergent cues** such as environment noise, phoneme-level artifacts, or spectral integrity.
- Validates the findings through **causal masking**, demonstrating that performance significantly degrades when the identified primary cues are removed from the signal.
key_results: Development of a localized attribution method for audio-native deepfake detection.
Identification of AASIST's reliance on non-speech and environmental artifacts rather than the speech itself.
Discovery that CA-MHFA specializes in detecting localized phoneme artifacts.
Verification that SLS focuses on word boundaries and spectral integrity cues.
Causal validation confirming that specific semantic regions identified are critical for model classification accuracy.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers reveal that deepfake speech detectors rely on diverse semantic cues like environment noise and phoneme artifacts.

title: Comparative Analysis of Inference-Time Defense Methods for Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) now appear in safety-critical applications, but the visual channel leaves them open to adversarial attacks that predominantly text-oriented safety alignment addresses only in part. Retraining a model for each new vulnerability class is usually too expensive to be practical. We report a comparative empirical evaluation of three inference-time defense methods and their combinations, run on eight models from the InternVL and Qwen-VL families across seven safety benchmarks that span four attack classes and total 9,000 evaluation samples. Every figure below comes from the same unified proxy classifier. Five findings emerge from the evaluation. First, within the evaluated models and benchmarks, no single defense dominates across all settings: what works depends on the model's baseline safety and on the attack type. Second, combining defenses directly drives benign-query over-refusal to 97-100% across all eight evaluated models, and SmoothVLM on its own reaches 99.2-100%. Third, a simple safety prompt keeps utility largely intact (0.0-18.2% over-refusal across all eight models, five of them below 7%, although two exceeded 15%) while still yielding moderate safety gains. Fourth, different attack classes expose different weaknesses across the evaluated setup, which is why multi-benchmark evaluation matters. Fifth, in a preliminary whitebox test on two models (n=20), text-level defenses suppressed a PGD visual attack that had succeeded without any defense: the defenses act at the output stage, where gradient optimization has limited direct leverage in the tested configuration. Read together, these results argue for adaptive defense selection rather than a single fixed defense configuration.

processed.summary:
- Evaluates **inference-time defense** mechanisms for **Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs)** to mitigate visual adversarial vulnerabilities without costly retraining.
- Utilizes a unified proxy classifier to compare **InternVL** and **Qwen-VL** model families across 9,000 evaluation samples and seven safety benchmarks.
- Identifies a critical trade-off between **adversarial robustness** and **model utility**, specifically highlighting how certain defenses trigger extreme **over-refusal** rates.
- Advocates for **adaptive defense selection** over fixed configurations, as defense efficacy varies significantly by model baseline and specific attack class.
key_results: No single defense method consistently dominates across all evaluated MLLM models and benchmarks.
Combining defenses or using SmoothVLM alone leads to nearly 100% over-refusal of benign queries.
Simple safety prompts maintain high utility (mostly <7% over-refusal) while providing moderate safety gains.
Multi-benchmark evaluation is essential because different attack classes expose unique model weaknesses.
Text-level defenses at the output stage can successfully suppress PGD visual attacks that bypass standard models.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that adaptive inference-time defenses effectively mitigate MLLM adversarial attacks while balancing safety and model utility.
